## Modelling, risk, and SPI-M-O

I've been reading the Orange Book on risk management in government.
It is prompting some parallels with SPI-M-O.

Firstly: where does SPI-M-O sit among the "Three Lines"?

This is a framework of "three lines [of defence]" for allocating responsibility for risk management across government:

1. Operational risk management (i.e. government department)
2. Internal review and oversight (internal audit)
3. Independent review

My suspicion is that SPI-M-O is "supposed" to be (3); was in practice closer to (2); occasionally went so far as (1), in the case of directly setting policy via R estimates.
I would be curious to hear how that aligns with the perception of SPI-M participants, intepreters, users, and the public.

Second: What is the purpose of SPI-M-O consensus statements - and what happens if we look at them against principles of "risk reporting"?

My current working hypothesis is that each statement is interpreted as a report on risk.
But this might not be how they are produced.
It might be interesting to contrast the statement text against standards for

1. Expert elicitation protocols (production perspective)
2. Risk reporting guidelines (user interpretation perspective), e.g. those in the [Orange Book](https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/611642cfe90e070541075731/Good_Practice_Guide_Risk_Reporting_Final.pdf)
